Having thought about this a bit more, perhaps we could do something similar to Theano's `warn_float64` configuration option: http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/library/config.html#config.warn_float64

i.e. allow users to set a flag so that a warning is raised when `get_all_params` is called without `trainable` set, and even for `get_output` without `deterministic` set.

I think this would have to be off by default though, otherwise we are again confronting people with library features that they might not be using / be interested in, which we should strive to avoid where possible.

The problem is then that there is no such thing as a Lasagne configuration file (and there probably shouldn't be?), so then it's a question of setting these flags programmatically at the top of your script, or something... which could easily be forgotten. That brings us back to our original problem. So yeah, this is tricky! 

Overall, I like the idea of using opt-in warnings to shield against this type of mistakes.

This gets extremely annoying for PGP keys. Files are typically encrypted by the PGP keys of all members of a team that has access to a file, so in the worst case you'll get prompted once for every key in the file. Prompting only before actually sending a successful response avoids this, as decrypting with your coworker's PGP keys will fail (and not prompt), but decrypting with your own PGP key will succeed (and prompt).

To fix this, you can make `request` an optional keyword argument and call the method like this:

```python
article = Article.objects.get(request=request, slug=slug, is_published=True)
```

This _should_ avoid breaking changes to existing calls to `Article.objects.get`.

It is probably _possible_ to go back in history and make this happen, but difficult.

More information: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1430749/getting-git-to-acknowledge-previously-moved-files

I'm wondering whether we should have this as an integration tests, rather than a unit test. I think using `test_request_context()` and *reading* from `flask.request` is generally fine (and a recommended pattern, or at least a ["testing trick"](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/testing/#other-testing-tricks) ), but manually *modifying* `flask.request` to invoke a certain code path that is usually triggered by a REST call seems like a bit of an anti-pattern.

One of the main problems I see with the flask unit testing approach is that LocalStack makes heavy use of proxies and invocation listeners (not the Lambda API currently, but other services are using it). So, if our testing is based on mocking flask requests, we might be potentially be missing (or skipping) parts of the service invocation chain. I'd rather not establish this as a pattern. 

I don't think it makes much sense to re-write the tests in this PR at this point (we can go ahead and merge as-is)
but I feel like this is something we should consider in the future.

They're reporting a ratio in relation to the worst accumulation type (wait/read/compute/write) in all stages.  The real measurement (in terms of milliseconds) will be the largest among all accumulation types in all stages, and becomes the denominator for all ratios.  It also means the slowest one has a 1.0 ratio, as numerator=denominator.
